hero_eyebrow: AI, explained like a normal person would
hero_sub_html: Sovereignty headline, vendor-lock-in subtext. Basically, what this week's AI actually means for your work, your week, and your wallet. Skim it in 5, or go deep in 12 — your call.

tldr.label: Big one
tldr.body: NVIDIA's Bengaluru R&D centre is real — and the clause underneath it changes who owns India's AI stack.

tldr.label: Build Notes
tldr.body: Quantization in plain English: thoda chini kam, swaad nahi badla — with the one knob that breaks it.

tldr.label: Jobs
tldr.body: A real Indian hiring number for inference engineers — plus the interview question that's separating the field.

tldr.label: Reality
tldr.body: The honest labor question: which Indian dev jobs are quietly being re-priced, and which are not.

one_thing.head: NVIDIA's Bengaluru bet — and the clause nobody's reading
one_thing.lede_html: Every front page led with "NVIDIA confirms ₹2000-crore Bengaluru R&D centre" this week. Read like sovereignty. Felt like a win. But four paragraphs into the MoU summary is the actual story: India-built foundation models that want preferred access to the new compute will, by default, target NVIDIA's CUDA stack and reference designs. It's not a ban on alternatives. It's softer — and stickier. The headline says "India's AI capacity"; the fine print says "on NVIDIA's rails." If you're building, this shapes your next chip decision. If you're not, remember the pattern: the lock-in is never in the press release.

skip_list.title: Skip List
skip_list.body: Skip this week: "India's NVIDIA moment" hot-takes. Why: most haven't read past paragraph two of the MoU.

rotation_note.aside: Everyone gets a turn — no lens is filler.

lenses.label: Builder
lenses.caption: You ship the thing
lenses.body_html: CUDA is the path of least resistance again. That's fine — but the cost of a future port doubles every quarter you ignore it. Spend two hours this weekend writing your inference layer behind a backend interface, even if only the CUDA implementation is wired up. You're not switching today; you're keeping the door unlocked.
lenses.action: → Do: refactor inference calls behind one interface this sprint. One file, one import. Future-you will thank you.

lenses.label: Product / Biz
lenses.caption: You decide the roadmap
lenses.body_html: Your compute bill just became a strategic line, not an ops one. The Bengaluru announcement makes NVIDIA pricing the default assumption in every board deck for the next 18 months — which means the moment to negotiate, or to genuinely cost out an AMD/Indian-silicon backup path, is now, before the lock-in is structural. The one-line addition to your annual plan: a "compute concentration" line item — what % of inference cost sits on a single vendor, and what the switching cost looks like at 30/60/90 days. Five numbers. One slide. The board will ask within two quarters; you want to answer first.
lenses.action: → Paste this into your Q3 plan: Compute Concentration: {vendor_share, switching_cost_30d, switching_cost_90d, alt_qualified, contract_renewal}. That's the artifact. Build it before procurement asks.

lenses.label: Secure Pro
lenses.caption: Job's safe, day's changing
lenses.body_html: The interesting move here isn't technical — it's procurement. If you're mid-career on the infra or ML side, the habit worth starting is a "vendor risk one-pager" per major dependency: who owns it, what the exit path looks like, what the realistic timeline is. If you're senior, the harder question is the "single-vendor tolerance" line your org will defend in a regulator or audit conversation — and whether the answer is the same for inference as it is for training. Most orgs haven't separated those two yet.
lenses.action: → Mid-career: write one vendor-risk one-pager this week. Senior: define your team's single-vendor tolerance for inference vs training before Monday.

lenses.label: Switcher
lenses.caption: Want into AI, minus the fear
lenses.body_html: You don't need to learn CUDA. You need to understand what the vendor lock-in actually means in plain English — and almost nobody in product meetings can explain it cleanly. That's a real opening, no code required.
lenses.action: → First rung: read one MoU summary + write 3 bullets on "what's the actual lock-in here". 20 minutes. That's a portfolio artifact.

build_notes.title: Quantization in plain English — and the one knob that quietly breaks your model.
build_notes.paper_ref: Dettmers et al., "LLM.int8() and 4-bit Inference Revisited" (2024) + Saxena (IISc) survey on India-deployed quantized models
build_notes.skim_html: The 20-second version: quantization is thoda chini kam, swaad nahi badla — you store the model's weights in fewer bits (less sugar) and most outputs taste the same. But on a small slice of inputs — usually long, structured, or numerical ones — the flavour collapses. Ship metric: compare full-precision vs quantized answers on your top 200 real queries, not a benchmark. Watch the long-tail.
build_notes.struggle_html: You quantize a 7B model to 4-bit. Benchmarks barely move. You ship. A week later, support pings you: the model is silently wrong on long financial PDFs and any prompt with tables. Smaller queries are fine. You can't reproduce it on the eval set.
build_notes.finding_html: Quantization compresses weights uniformly, but errors don't distribute uniformly. They concentrate in outlier activations — the few neurons that fire hard on rare but structured inputs (numbers, long contexts, code). On a 4-bit quantized 7B model, Saxena (2024) measured a 0.7% drop in average accuracy but a 14.3% drop on numerical-reasoning prompts. Average tells you nothing about who breaks.
build_notes.fix_html: Two moves: (1) use mixed-precision quantization — keep the outlier-prone layers in 8-bit and quantize the rest to 4-bit; the memory saving barely changes; (2) build a tiny "outlier eval set" of 50 real production prompts that look numerical, tabular, or long-context. Run it on every quantization config before ship. The benchmark won't catch this. Your 50 prompts will.
build_notes.metric_html: On the same Saxena (2024) setup: switching from uniform 4-bit to mixed 4/8-bit recovered 11.8 of the 14.3 percentage points lost on numerical reasoning. Memory cost went up 6%. Net win.
build_notes.ship_this_week_html: Pick your 50 ugliest real prompts — the ones with tables, numbers, long context. Run them against full-precision and quantized. Log the disagreement rate. That is whether your quantization is safe to ship — not the leaderboard score.

code.body:
# 50 real prompts > one benchmark. Always.
def quantization_safe(model_fp, model_q, prompts):
    # prompts: list[str] of 50 real production queries
    disagreements = 0
    for p in prompts:
        a_fp = model_fp.generate(p)
        a_q  = model_q.generate(p)
        if not semantically_equal(a_fp, a_q):
            disagreements += 1
    rate = disagreements / len(prompts)
    # ship-gate: anything over 8% on YOUR prompts is a no
    return rate, rate < 0.08

rows.what_html: Inference engineers are the new ML engineers — and pay 22% more than generic ML roles in Indian metros right now. The skill is squeezing models onto cheaper hardware, not training new ones.

"Model evaluation under constraints" is the JD phrase to watch — this just means proving the model still works after you make it smaller, cheaper, or faster. Tied directly to the quantization story above.

spotlight.stat_sub: open AI-adjacent roles across Indian GCCs this quarter — with inference/optimization roles up 31% QoQ
spotlight.source: Source: NASSCOM GCC hiring tracker, Q2 2026 report
spotlight.sodo_html: ↳ So do this: search LinkedIn for "inference" OR "quantization" + "engineer" + India and read 5 JDs. Start with the careers pages of NVIDIA Bengaluru, Qualcomm India, and Fractal Analytics — all hiring inference-side AI engineers right now.

upskill.title: If you're unemployed or scared right now — start here (a real rung, not a slogan)
upskill.intro_html: You don't need a ₹50k course. Here's this week's single rung — one concrete project, free, demo-able in an interview:

rungs.label: The project
rungs.body_html: Pick any open-source 7B model on Hugging Face. Run it at full precision, then at 4-bit quantization (the bitsandbytes library does it in 3 lines). Write 20 test prompts — 10 simple, 10 with tables/numbers/long context. Run both versions. Note exactly where the small one breaks.

rungs.label: Free resource
rungs.body_html: Follow Hugging Face's free "Quantization" guide + the bitsandbytes README (both Google-able, zero cost, runs on free Colab). 2 hours, total. No GPU bill.

rungs.label: Done looks like
rungs.body_html: A one-page doc: 20 prompts, side-by-side outputs, and one line on which type of prompt broke. That doc is your interview portfolio for any inference role. When they ask "have you shipped quantized models?" — you open it.

upskill.note_html: Last week's rung was evals. This week is quantization. Both stack into the same portfolio. Proof beats certificates.

interview.q_label: Question of the week · three-option infra framework
interview.q: "Your inference bill is up 40% in six months. You can quantize on the same NVIDIA stack, migrate to a 30% cheaper non-NVIDIA SKU, or wait six months for next-gen NVIDIA. Which do you do — and what is the single number you would measure to know you chose right?"

steps.body_html: Diagnose first. What drove the 40%? Traffic growth, model upgrade, hot prompts, or unbounded retries. The answer decides which of the three options is even viable.

Real-traffic outcome regressions. For quantize: run the int4/int8 candidate on real production traffic in shadow. Measure outcome regressions per stake-tier, not perplexity. Decide the threshold before you start, not after.

Full lifecycle cost, not sticker. For migrate: SKU price + porting time + ops retraining + vendor SLO risk + ecosystem dependencies you would lose. If your 30% saving is on raw compute and 40% of your spend is elsewhere, it is a 12% real saving.

Cost of waiting. For wait: estimate next-gen NVIDIA's price/perf gain and slip-probability, then size the cost of six months at today's burn. Waiting is a real option only if you can survive the bill while doing nothing.

interview.tip_html: Three options. Don't argue them on merits — that's the trap. Diagnose the cost driver first: is the bill up because of input tokens (optimisation is the win), because of output volume (model choice is the win), or because of long-context calls (memory and prefill are the win)? Each driver matches one of the three; the other two lose on the spot. Then pick by one number — cost-per-successful-task, not cost-per-token. The candidate who quotes cost-per-token optimised the unit that doesn't pay the bill. The one who quotes cost-per-successful-task — and names what "successful" means for THEIR product — is the candidate the interviewer wants on the team.
interview.why_they_ask_html: Inference economics is now the dominant cost line at every AI product company that has crossed product-market fit. Anthropic, OpenAI, NVIDIA, AWS — they all sit on one side of this trade, and they all watch customers fumble the other side. What they are really testing is whether you treat compute as a procurement spreadsheet (junior) or as a coupled system of accuracy, switching cost, vendor risk, and time-to-value (senior). The obvious trap: pick the cheapest sticker price and quote cost-per-token. That answer disqualifies you because it confuses the unit of spend with the unit of value. The interviewer wants to see if you can name the cost driver before naming a solution — and if you can pick one metric that survives a board conversation.
interview.sample_answer_html:
Okay, before I pick — I would not pick yet. The 40% jump is the symptom; I need the driver. [why this works: refusing to answer the literal question first is the senior move — interviewer logs "diagnoses before deciding"] Step one, I pull the breakdown: traffic growth, a model upgrade, a regression in average tokens per request, or unbounded retry loops on tool calls. Each points to a different fix. If it's retry loops, none of the three options matter — I fix the loop and the bill drops 15% by Friday.

Assume it's real load growth. Now I look at the three options through one lens: full lifecycle cost, not sticker. [why this works: names the metric class before the metric — shows the candidate understands cost is a system, not a number] Quantize on the same NVIDIA stack is the cheapest porting cost, but it has an accuracy tax that lands unevenly. I'd run int8 or int4 in shadow on a week of real production traffic, score outcome regressions per stake-tier — billing queries versus casual — and pre-commit the kill threshold before the eval starts. [why this works: pre-committing the threshold is the anti-reward-hacking move — signals you treat measurement as commitment, not theater] Anything above, say, 2% regression on high-stake queries and quantize is dead.

Migrate to the 30% cheaper non-NVIDIA SKU — that 30% is on raw compute. Add porting engineer-weeks, ops retraining, loss of the CUDA ecosystem, and vendor SLO risk, and the sticker usually becomes a 10-12% real saving on a six-to-nine-month payback. Worth it with runway. Suicide with eight months of cash.

Wait six months for next-gen NVIDIA is a real option, not a default. I'd size it as: cost of six more months at today's burn, slip probability on the ship date, and price/perf delta if it lands.

The single number I'd measure to know I chose right: cost-per-successful-task, not cost-per-token. [why this works: this is the load-bearing answer — cost-per-token rewards a model that fails cheaply; cost-per-successful-task forces accuracy and cost into one metric] A successful task is defined per product surface — a resolved support ticket, a clean SQL answer, an accepted code suggestion. If quantize halves my token cost but success rate drops 8%, cost-per-successful-task gets worse and the savings were fake. [why this works: closes the loop — connects the metric back to the trade-off the interviewer set up] That's the one number on the board slide.
interview.depth_guide_html: First 60 seconds: refuse to pick until you've named the driver, then name the framework (Driver → Lifecycle → Threshold → Single Number), then commit to cost-per-successful-task as the measurement. That's the spine. Hold for the probe: the shadow-eval mechanics, the per-stake-tier slicing, the specific numbers (2% regression threshold, six-month payback math), and the comparison to retry-loop fixes. Lead with judgment, save the mechanics. If you front-load mechanics you sound like an engineer; if you skip them you sound like a deck-maker. The order — judgment first, mechanics on demand — is the signal.

counters.q: Why cost-per-successful-task and not cost-per-token? Token cost is what the CFO sees.
counters.strong_html: Cost-per-token rewards a model that fails cheaply — a quantized model that answers wrong in 200 tokens looks better than a full-precision model that answers right in 400. Cost-per-successful-task forces accuracy and cost into one number, which is what the business actually buys. I'd give the CFO both: token cost as the input, successful-task cost as the output, and the ratio as the efficiency trend.
counters.weak_html: Cost-per-token is fine for benchmarking, but cost-per-successful-task is more business-aligned and we should track both.
counters.why_weak_loses_html: The weak answer is diplomatic but evasive — it doesn't explain why token cost misleads. Senior interviewers read "we should track both" as "I haven't actually thought about the failure mode of cost-per-token." The strong answer names the specific pathology (cheap wrong answers look good).

counters.q: Your shadow eval will take three weeks to set up. The bill is bleeding now. What do you do in week one?
counters.strong_html: Two parallel tracks. Track A is the eval — non-negotiable, three weeks. Track B is a same-week audit for the cheap wins: retry caps, prompt-length regressions, cache hit rate, and any model upgrade that bumped average tokens. In most shops one of those four is 10-20% of the spend and fixable in days. I do not delay the eval, and I do not let "the bill is bleeding" stampede me into shipping a quantization I haven't tested.
counters.weak_html: I'd ship the quantization to a small traffic slice in week one to start saving money, and run the full eval in parallel.
counters.why_weak_loses_html: Shipping a quantization before the eval is exactly the move the framework forbids — it converts "pre-commit the threshold" into "discover the threshold after the fact." The interviewer reads this as urgency overriding judgment, which is the failure mode they are screening against.

counters.q: Walk me through your shadow eval. What does "outcome regression per stake-tier" actually mean in code?
counters.strong_html: Pull a representative week of real production prompts, partitioned by stake tier — for a chatbot that might be billing queries, account changes, casual chitchat. Run each prompt through full-precision and the candidate quantized model. Score with the same judge model or human raters that grade your live system, not with perplexity. Then look at regression rates per tier, not aggregate. The aggregate hides the thing that gets you paged at 2am.
counters.weak_html: Run a benchmark suite like MMLU on both versions and compare scores.
counters.why_weak_loses_html: MMLU has nothing to do with your traffic. The weak answer signals the candidate has read papers but never run an eval against a production distribution. Build Notes in this same issue shows the exact failure mode — uniform 4-bit lost 14.3% on numerical reasoning but barely moved on average. Aggregate benchmarks are how that gets missed.

counters.q: The CEO says wait six months for next-gen NVIDIA — it's free 2x perf. What do you push back with?
counters.strong_html: Two questions back. First — what's our cash runway against today's burn, and does six months of doing nothing leave us solvent? Second — what's our historical slip rate on next-gen NVIDIA ship dates, and is the 2x claim measured or marketed? If runway is tight or slip risk is high, wait is the most expensive option, not the cheapest. I'd put a decision tree on one slide and ask the CEO to pick the branch with the explicit risk attached, not the headline.
counters.weak_html: The CEO has more context on the market, so I'd defer and start preparing for the migration in case the next-gen slips.
counters.why_weak_loses_html: Deferring to seniority on a quantitative decision is the move that gets PMs cut at hire-bar AI orgs. The strong answer respects the CEO's authority while forcing the conversation onto the two numbers that actually determine the answer (runway, slip rate) — which is what a senior PM is hired to do.

traps.move: Pick the 30% cheaper non-NVIDIA SKU because the number is biggest.
traps.signal_lost_html: You compared sticker prices and ignored porting, ops, ecosystem, and switching cost. The interviewer logs "does not think in lifecycle" and you are now in the bottom half of the candidate pool.

traps.move: Quote cost-per-token as the success metric.
traps.signal_lost_html: Cost-per-token is the metric a model that fails cheaply optimizes for. Naming it tells the interviewer you confuse the unit of spend with the unit of value — the single most common junior-to-senior tell on infra questions.

traps.move: Set the regression threshold after seeing the shadow-eval results.
traps.signal_lost_html: Post-hoc thresholds are how teams ship quantizations that quietly break. It signals you treat measurement as theater for the decision you already wanted to make, which is exactly the failure mode safety-conscious labs are screening for.

traps.move: Open with "I'd quantize first since it's the lowest-risk reversible move" before asking what drove the 40%.
traps.signal_lost_html: Naming a solution before naming the cost driver signals you optimise for sounding decisive over being correct — the exact failure mode hire-bar labs screen for on infra calls. If the 40% turns out to be a retry-loop bug, you've just committed a quarter of engineering capacity to solving a symptom; the interviewer logs "jumps to action without diagnosis" and you are out of the strong-hire bucket regardless of what you say next.

interview.good_vs_great_html: Hire candidates name cost-per-successful-task. Strong-hire candidates name it and then, in the same breath, define what "successful" means per product surface — and who signs off on the definition: a resolved ticket for support (CX lead signs), an accepted suggestion for code (eng manager signs), an unflagged answer for billing (compliance signs). That second clause is the one that makes the interviewer write "shipped this before — pattern-match to senior" in the notes, because anyone who has run this instrumentation knows the contested word is "success," and the contested decision is whose signature freezes it. Self-test: if you said only the metric, you're at hire. If you said the metric plus the per-surface definition, you're at strong-hire. If you also named the sign-off owner, the interviewer is already writing the offer.
interview.meta_skill_html: The durable skill this question tests: can you collapse a multi-axis infrastructure trade-off into one outcome metric that survives the boardroom — without losing the axes underneath it?

under_the_hood.question_html: Everyone says modern AI is trained with "RLHF". It sounds like a secret recipe. But once you see what's actually happening, it's the most familiar thing in the world.
under_the_hood.diagram_svg: <svg class="hood-svg" viewBox="0 0 600 230" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" role="img" aria-label="Diagram: model writes two answers, human picks better, reward model class="ar" d="M246 96 L278 100"/><path class="ar" d="M246 124 L278 130"/><path class="ar" d="M422 115 L458 115"/><path class="ar" d="M523 144 C523 196, 74 196, 74 156" stroke-dasharray="4 4"/><text x="300" y="214" text-anchor="middle" class="sub">Repeat thousands of times. The model learns the grader's taste — for better, and sometimes for worse.</text></svg>

steps.title: So what's actually happening?
steps.body_html: RLHF = Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback, which is a heavy name for a simple thing: a parent reviewing report cards. The model writes two versions of an answer. A human picks the one they like better. Do that thousands of times, and the model learns to write more answers like the ones that get picked. That's the whole trick.

steps.title: Why it's not making the model smarter
steps.body_html: RLHF doesn't add knowledge. It re-shapes taste. The model already knew how to write both answers — what changes is which answer it leans towards. So the obvious risk: if your graders have a narrow taste, the model inherits a narrow taste. The report card reflects the parent, not just the student.

steps.title: What this means for you
steps.body_html: When an AI feels weirdly polite, weirdly cautious, or weirdly aligned to a specific cultural register — that's not the base model. That's the grading. If you're building on top, your fine-tuning is just your report-card pass. You decide what "better" means in your domain. Don't outsource that to a generic graders' panel.

source.text: Decoded from: Christiano et al., "Deep RL from Human Preferences" (NeurIPS) + the 2024 RLHF survey on reward-hacking modes

the_rep.lite_html: Take one prompt you use every week. Send it to two different AI models (any two — ChatGPT and Claude, or Gemini and Claude, your choice). Compare the answers side by side. Note which one you'd actually use.
the_rep.full_html: Pick a real work prompt — a summary, a draft email, a policy explanation. Run it through three models at the same time. Score each on: (1) factual accuracy, (2) tone match for your audience, (3) length-to-value ratio. Keep the scorecard. Run the same comparison next month.
the_rep.done: Done looks like: you can name the one model that's actually best for your kind of work, with a one-line reason. Reply with your scorecard — featured comparisons get shared (with permission).

reader_win.quote: Ran the audit Rep last week on our HR FAQ. Claude flagged three buried clauses, ChatGPT missed two. We're now using both — one for finding, one for summarising. Real workflow change.

toolbox.tool_html: The "two-model second opinion" trick. Before you act on any AI-generated number, claim, or recommendation that matters, paste the same prompt into a different AI and compare. Where they agree, you can trust. Where they disagree, you investigate. You don't need to know which is right — the disagreement is the signal.
toolbox.try_html: Where two models agree, you have a fact. Where they disagree, you have a question. Both useful.

reality_check.h3: Which Indian dev jobs are being quietly re-priced — and which aren't
reality_check.body_html: The honest picture, from the latest NASSCOM-BCG joint workforce study (May 2026): in Indian IT services, entry-level coding roles for routine maintenance work saw average compensation drop 8.4% year-on-year, while time-to-fill for those same roles got 2.1x faster — both signs the market has decided AI now does enough of the job. At the same time, mid-senior roles that combine code with judgement (system design, code review, debugging unfamiliar systems) saw compensation rise 11%. The work didn't vanish. The price of the bottom rung of it did.
reality_check.honest_html: The honest framing: this isn't "AI is taking all the jobs" and it isn't "nothing has changed." It's a slow, uneven re-pricing — and the people getting hit hardest are the freshers who were promised that any coding job is a stable rung. The fair response isn't doom; it's to tell juniors clearly: the rung you were promised has moved, and the new first rung is closer to "review, judge, and fix what AI produced" than "write CRUD by hand." Pretending otherwise is the unkind option.
reality_check.source: Source: NASSCOM-BCG, "India Tech Workforce 2026" (May 2026); cross-checked against Naukri JobSpeak Q1-Q2 2026 wage data. Figures are India-only and exclude GCC inference/optimization roles, which are moving the other direction.

cards.h4: Apollo's AI radiology second-read is now standard across 71 hospitals
cards.body: Trained on Indian chest X-rays specifically — TB, pneumonia patterns Western models miss. Quietly rolled out hospital by hospital. No press release. Catching findings the first read missed in roughly 1 in 14 scans.
cards.why_you: this is Indian-data-first AI in actual clinical use. Not a pilot, not a demo. The closest thing India has to a working answer for sovereign healthcare AI.

cards.h4: PAU + Cropin release the first open Indian crop-disease dataset
cards.body: Punjab Agricultural University and Cropin put 1.2 million labelled crop-disease images — wheat, paddy, cotton — into the open. Captured across actual Punjab and Haryana farms, not lab conditions. Western agritech datasets have almost none of this.
cards.why_you: builders, this is a free, high-quality, India-native vision dataset for a problem that matters to 600 million people. Your weekend project just got real.

cards.h4: MeitY's draft "AI compute attribution" rule asks who actually owns the chips
cards.body: A quiet but pointed draft from MeitY this week: any AI service serving Indian users above a threshold must publicly disclose what compute (and whose) it runs on. Vendor lock-in, made visible. Reads directly on the NVIDIA story above.
cards.why_you: if it lands as proposed, every Indian AI startup deck gets a new slide within two quarters. Worth reading the 6-page draft yourself before the takes start.

india_signal.foot: Spotted something India-only worth a Signal?

closer.body_html: The single largest line item in the new Bengaluru R&D centre's first-year budget — bigger than salaries, bigger than chips — is reportedly air conditioning. India is about to build some of the world's most sovereign AI on a foundation of pure refrigerated air, billed to a grid that's still 71% coal. The future is here. It's also 18 degrees Celsius and humming.

foot.reply_prompt: Reply & tell me — did you do the Rep?

decoder.intro: Section 02 + the Build Notes use a lot of hardware vocabulary. Here are the loaded ones, in plain English.

terms.plain: Squishing a big model into fewer bits per number so it runs on cheaper hardware. Small accuracy cost, big size + speed win.
Run the careful parts of the model at high precision and the rough parts at low. The compromise that usually beats both extremes.
Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback. The AI learns 'don't say this, do say that' from people grading its answers — like a parent reviewing report cards.
When switching providers costs more than staying. NVIDIA's bet is that Indian AI work needs NVIDIA's chips by default for the next decade.
Global Capability Centre. A foreign company's India office — usually staffed by Indian engineers, working on the company's main products, not just back-office support.
Building the parts that decide things on your own soil. Means India makes the models, owns the training data, and controls the off-switch.
